provenance: Synthesized from 'Mobile-First Vertical Microdrama Visual Direction and Evaluation Framework' (input-research/, pages 19-24) under the 8 founder rulings of 2 Jul 2026. Garbled anchor rows were re-verified against the PDF via word-coordinate extraction (ruling 7); every deviation is documented in SYNTHESIS_NOTES.md. Anchor levels 1/3/5/7/10 carry the source's verbatim keyword; levels 2/4/6/8/9 are interpolated and flagged _derived.

parameters.name: First-frame hook
parameters.definition: The opening image's ability to stop a scroll or hold entry: instant conflict, a face, danger, or urgent proof.

1.text: Neutral opening - the first frame carries no signal at all; nothing invites a stop.

2.text: Faint signal - a subject exists but no tension, question, or urgency reads.

3.text: Slow or generic - the opening image resembles a thousand others or takes seconds to mean anything.

4.text: Mildly engaging - one element (face, prop, framing) earns a beat of attention.

5.text: Readable but not urgent - the situation is instantly legible, yet nothing presses the viewer to stay.

6.text: Engaging - legible plus one urgent element; most viewers pause.

7.text: Urgent and clear - conflict or danger reads within the first second and demands the next.

8.text: Very strong - an arresting, specific image that opens a question on sight.

9.text: Near-unforgettable - a scroll-stopping frame a hair short of iconic.

10.text: Unforgettable opening image - instantly legible, emotionally loaded, and unlike anything adjacent in the feed.

parameters.name: Visual story clarity
parameters.definition: Ability to understand who, what, and why from the images: cause and effect legible without leaning on dialogue.

1.text: Viewers lost - who, what, or why cannot be followed from the images at all.

2.text: Heavily obscured - the spine is guessable only with effort and rewinding.

3.text: Frequent ambiguity - key beats are repeatedly unclear; proof shots missing.

4.text: Patchy - most beats read but at least one pivotal moment is visually mute.

5.text: Mostly clear - the story reads, with occasional soft spots a caption has to rescue.

6.text: Clear - cause and effect read on first viewing; minor polish possible.

7.text: Clear with minor gaps - visually self-sufficient storytelling with only trivial ambiguities.

8.text: Very clear - every beat lands visually; inserts and reactions carry the causality.

9.text: Near-crystal - the muted episode would still be fully understood; one soft frame remains.

10.text: Crystal clear - the images alone tell the complete story; no viewer, in any language, is ever lost.

parameters.name: Vertical composition
parameters.definition: How well the frame uses 9:16: subject centered, depth, clean tiering.

1.text: Frame fights format - horizontal staging crammed into 9:16; subjects cut off or lost.

2.text: Poor fit - the portrait frame is tolerated, not used; constant awkward headroom or dead zones.

3.text: Frequent empty edges - large unused frame areas; the vertical field wasted more often than not.

4.text: Serviceable-minus - subjects sit correctly but depth and tiering are rare.

5.text: Competent - clean, centered vertical framing throughout; conventional but correct.

6.text: Good - deliberate use of vertical depth in several shots.

7.text: Strong portrait use - foreground/background tiering and vertical lines actively composed.

8.text: Very strong - the vertical frame shapes meaning: dominance, isolation, proximity.

9.text: Near-native - composition thinks phone-first in nearly every shot.

10.text: Frame feels native to phone - 9:16 is the film language, not a crop; every composition exploits the format.

parameters.name: Emotional readability
parameters.definition: Facial and bodily feeling transmission: reactions decode instantly on a small screen.

1.text: Emotions unreadable - faces too far, too dark, or too flat to decode.

2.text: Barely readable - emotion is present but the camera keeps missing it.

3.text: Effortful - the viewer works to infer feeling; key reactions under-shot.

4.text: Inconsistent - some beats land emotionally, others go blank.

5.text: Mostly legible - primary emotions read, nuance does not.

6.text: Legible - feelings read reliably in close-ups and holds.

7.text: Easy to read - every key emotion lands instantly at phone size.

8.text: Very strong - emotion is staged, lit, and held so it cannot be missed.

9.text: Near-exceptional - immediate and starting to layer (mixed feelings readable).

10.text: Immediate and layered - feelings decode in a glance yet carry depth; reaction shots do dramatic work of their own.

parameters.name: Reveal execution
parameters.definition: The reveal is visible, timed, and emotionally landed: proof + reaction + consequence.

1.text: Reveal unreadable - the audience cannot see or grasp the evidence that turns the story.

2.text: Nearly lost - the reveal exists on screen but most viewers would miss it.

3.text: Late or confusing - the proof arrives at the wrong beat or reads only on second viewing.

4.text: Under-executed - visible but flat; reaction or consequence shot missing.

5.text: Functional - proof shown and understood; the moment lands without force.

6.text: Solid - proof plus reaction present and timed; consequence implied.

7.text: Strong - proof, reaction, and consequence all land in sequence at the right beat.

8.text: Very strong - the reveal is staged for maximum legibility and emotional impact.

9.text: Near-devastating - a fully weaponized reveal one cut from perfect.

10.text: Devastatingly clear - an unmissable, perfectly timed reveal whose proof, reaction, and consequence hit like a verdict.

parameters.name: Blocking and power geometry
parameters.definition: Social rank made visible in space: foreground ownership, height, doors, distance.

1.text: Random staging - actor placement carries no meaning; power invisible.

2.text: Accidental - occasional meaningful placement, clearly unplanned.

3.text: Weak hierarchy - rank is sometimes suggested but equal framing flattens unequal stakes.

4.text: Intermittent - deliberate geometry in a few scenes only.

5.text: Some rank visible - status reads in key confrontations, generic elsewhere.

6.text: Consistent - placement, height, and distance track the power map scene to scene.

7.text: Strong status shifts - power reversals are staged spatially and read without a word.

8.text: Very strong - geometry anticipates and amplifies every beat change.

9.text: Near-self-sufficient - blocking alone nearly narrates the drama.

10.text: Geometry tells story by itself - the spatial arrangement alone communicates who holds power and when it moves.

parameters.name: Visual escalation
parameters.definition: The episode intensifies visually: scale, movement, density, stakes rise together.

1.text: Flat line - the last minute looks like the first; zero visual build.

2.text: Trace build - one intensifying element, quickly abandoned.

3.text: Weak build - intent to escalate is visible but the rise keeps stalling.

4.text: Uneven - escalation in patches with visual plateaus between.

5.text: Some build - a readable rise exists, gentle and partly accidental.

6.text: Structured - a deliberate ladder with most rungs in place.

7.text: Clear ladder - scale, pace, and density step up beat by beat toward the climax.

8.text: Very strong - the escalation is felt physically; each beat visibly outbids the last.

9.text: Near-relentless - continuous coherent rise with a single soft step.

10.text: Relentless but coherent rise - unbroken visual escalation that never tips into noise.

parameters.name: Cliffhanger image strength
parameters.definition: The terminal image's continuation pull: an unanswered image question that creates purchase or next-click pressure.

1.text: No pull - the episode ends on a resolved or empty frame; nothing asks to be continued.

2.text: Faint pull - a residual mood, no visual question.

3.text: Soft stop - the ending frame gestures at more but releases the tension before the cut.

4.text: Mild - a question exists in dialogue but the final image undersells it.

5.text: Serviceable - the final frame holds a readable open question.

6.text: Firm - a specific visual question at the cut; the next tap is likely.

7.text: Compelling - the terminal image freezes an unresolved action or threat at its peak.

8.text: Very strong - image and timing engineered so stopping feels like a loss.

9.text: Near-irresistible - a purchase-pressure ending one frame from perfect.

10.text: Irresistible - the final image is an unbearable open question; continuation (or payment) feels inevitable.

parameters.name: Edit rhythm
parameters.definition: The cut pattern suits beat density and readability: pace aligns to duration.

1.text: Incomprehensible or dull - cutting so fast it scrambles, or so slow it dies.

2.text: Erratic - pace lurches without relation to the drama.

3.text: Unstable - stretches of overcut or drag; rhythm fights the beats.

4.text: Rough - workable pace with recurring mistimed cuts.

5.text: Acceptable - conventional rhythm that neither helps nor hurts.

6.text: Assured - cuts land on beats; holds appear where emotion needs them.

7.text: Controlled - rhythm consciously shaped to duration and beat density.

8.text: Very strong - the cut pattern itself builds tension and release.

9.text: Near-addictive - propulsive and legible; one sequence from perfect.

10.text: Addictive and clear - a rhythm that pulls the viewer through while every frame stays readable.

parameters.name: Shot economy
parameters.definition: Each shot advances value: no wasted coverage.

1.text: Bloated and redundant - coverage padding everywhere; shots repeat without adding.

2.text: Heavy - noticeable filler in most scenes.

3.text: Repeated same value - multiple shots doing one shot's job.

4.text: Loose - occasional redundancy; trims available in every scene.

5.text: Acceptable - functional coverage with modest excess.

6.text: Lean-ish - most shots earn their place.

7.text: Efficient - every shot advances information, emotion, or pressure.

8.text: Very efficient - compression adds energy; nothing removable without loss.

9.text: Near-razor - essentially minimal; one shot in question.

10.text: Razor-sharp - the minimum set of shots, each indispensable, each multiplying value.

parameters.name: Performance capture
parameters.definition: Camera and editorial use of acting: take choice, hold length, framing.

1.text: Best emotion missed - the camera or cut consistently abandons the performance at its peak.

2.text: Poor - strong moments exist in the footage logic but rarely survive to screen.

3.text: Partial capture - key beats caught, others cut away too early or framed too wide.

4.text: Uneven - good instincts, inconsistent execution across scenes.

5.text: Enough - performances read; holds and take choices are safe.

6.text: Good - deliberate holds on the right faces at the right beats.

7.text: Strong - take selection, hold length, and framing consistently serve the acting.

8.text: Very strong - editorial choices actively elevate the performances.

9.text: Near-maximized - the screen extracts nearly everything the actors gave.

10.text: Performance maximized - camera and cut squeeze full value from every take; nothing an actor did well is wasted.

parameters.name: Sound and music effectiveness
parameters.definition: Dialogue clear, music helps: clean speech, smart stings, use of silence.

1.text: Speech unintelligible - dialogue materially cannot be understood.

2.text: Badly compromised - constant strain to hear; music fights speech.

3.text: Masking frequent - score or effects repeatedly bury lines.

4.text: Uneven - clean in places, muddy in others; stings generic.

5.text: Usable - dialogue clear; music present but decorative.

6.text: Supportive - the mix is clean and cues land with the beats.

7.text: Effective - sound design shapes tension; silence used deliberately.

8.text: Very strong - audio actively drives emotion without ever costing a word.

9.text: Near-exceptional - impactful and pristine; one cue from perfect.

10.text: High-impact and clean - a mix that hits hard, stays clear, and makes silence count.

parameters.name: Subtitle and UI safety
parameters.definition: Key information never blocked by text or app chrome: caption and UI safe zones honored.

1.text: Captions hide story - crucial evidence or action sits under captions or app UI.

2.text: Repeated blocking - story-relevant material obscured more than once.

3.text: Frequent collisions - text and UI regularly overlap faces, props, or on-screen text.

4.text: Occasional collisions - a few avoidable overlaps, none fatal.

5.text: Mostly safe - safe zones generally respected; near-misses remain.

6.text: Safe - no meaningful collisions; placement is competent.

7.text: Safe and planned - framing anticipated captions and chrome from the shoot onward.

8.text: Very strong - proof shots and text elements composed around the caption band by design.

9.text: Near-elegant - safety invisible because it was designed in everywhere.

10.text: Integrated elegantly - captions, UI, and imagery composed as one; nothing ever collides, nothing feels constrained.

parameters.name: Production feasibility
parameters.definition: Visual ambition fits resources: shootability and coverage practicality.

1.text: Impossible - the plan cannot be executed on a microdrama schedule or budget.

2.text: Improbable - executable only with luck or overrun baked in.

3.text: Likely overrun - too many moves, locations, or setups for the slot.

4.text: Tight - feasible with visible strain and no slack for problems.

5.text: Achievable with strain - the package fits, but only just.

6.text: Manageable - modest slack; the plan absorbs a bad day.

7.text: Realistic - ambition and resources are honestly matched.

8.text: Very efficient - the design gets more screen value than its budget suggests.

9.text: Near-elegant - almost maximal value per setup.

10.text: Elegantly efficient - a lean package engineered to look richer than it costs.

parameters.name: Localization and compliance fit
parameters.definition: Visuals fit the region and avoid avoidable risk: culturally coherent props, gestures, rituals.

1.text: Severe mismatch or risk - imagery that offends, misrepresents, or breaches platform/regulatory lines for the market.

2.text: High friction - multiple cultural misfires or compliance exposures.

3.text: Notable friction - stereotype or taboo collisions a local viewer would flag.

4.text: Minor friction - small mismatches in props, gestures, or rituals.

5.text: Adequate - nothing offends; nothing feels specifically local either.

6.text: Fitting - visual culture mostly reads native; compliance clean.

7.text: Good fit - props, gestures, and rituals are coherent for the region; no avoidable risk.

8.text: Very good - local visual texture does storytelling work.

9.text: Near-native - a local viewer would assume a local crew; negligible risk.

10.text: Deeply native feel - the imagery belongs to the market completely, with zero compliance exposure.

composites.name: Overall Visual Score
composites.formulaText: OVS = SUM(score_i x effectiveWeight_i) / 10 over all 15 parameters (weights sum to 100; equivalent to the weighted mean x 10).

composites.name: Commercial Visual Pull
composites.formulaText: CVP = 10 x (0.22 x first_frame_hook + 0.14 x emotional_readability + 0.14 x visual_escalation + 0.18 x reveal_execution + 0.20 x cliffhanger_image + 0.12 x edit_rhythm).

composites.name: Visual Craft Score
composites.formulaText: VCS = 10 x (0.18 x vertical_composition + 0.20 x visual_story_clarity + 0.16 x blocking_power_geometry + 0.12 x shot_economy + 0.14 x performance_capture + 0.10 x sound_music + 0.10 x subtitle_ui_safety).

composites.name: Production Feasibility Score
composites.formulaText: PFS = 10 x (0.35 x production_feasibility + 0.20 x shot_economy + 0.20 x blocking_power_geometry + 0.15 x edit_rhythm + 0.10 x subtitle_ui_safety).

composites.name: Localization and Compliance Risk
composites.formulaText: LCR = 100 - 10 x (0.45 x localization_compliance + 0.25 x subtitle_ui_safety + 0.15 x visual_story_clarity + 0.15 x sound_music). INVERTED: higher is worse; <= 30 is good. If local counsel or platform policy flags a serious issue, LCR is floored at 70 (source override rule).

priority.formulaText: Priority_i = effectiveWeight_i x (10 - score_i) x contextMultiplier_i; top items are the fix-first list. Multipliers mirror the script rubric's 1.5 emphasis (S1 p.30) onto the visual hook/cliffhanger - a Rubric v1 derivation, S2 has no priority formula (see SYNTHESIS_NOTES).

confidence.formulaText: Confidence = 0.45 x InputCompleteness + 0.25 x EvidenceDensity + 0.20 x ModeAppropriateness + 0.10 x EvaluatorCertainty (each component 0-100). Components operationalized in Rubric v1 - the source names but never defines them (see SYNTHESIS_NOTES).

components.note: 100 x (valid calls / expected calls); rejected or evidence-less calls reduce it.

gates.description: First-frame hook <= 3 on an Episode 1 or free-block chapter.
Reveal execution <= 3 when the reveal is the episode's function.
Subtitle/UI safety <= 3: captions or app chrome cover crucial story information.
Visual story clarity <= 3 in rough-cut or finished-episode mode.
Production feasibility <= 3 in plan mode (unreachable in v1; shipped for completeness).
Localization/compliance fit <= 2 in a region-specific release (note the source's deliberate lower threshold).
Cliffhanger image <= 3 on a pre-paywall or continuation-dependent (non-finale) episode.
Local counsel or platform policy flags a serious issue (also floors LCR at 70).

modes.note: The only reachable visual mode in v1 (video upload). All base weights; no mode deltas (source: 'all base weights; no major reduction').
Source mode; no UI path in v1.
Source mode (shot list); no UI path in v1.
Source mode; in v1 the script rubric owns the text path.

weightShift.note: Founder ruling 5 applied to the source's magnitude-less 'additive deltas' instruction: +/-20% relative, multiplicative stacking, renormalized to 100, shifted weights flagged derived.

gateCap.note: Founder rulings 1 and 6: a triggered gate blocks both Ready labels and resolves to the most specific repair action - the final label is the WORSE of the numeric ladder band (recut / reject tier) and the most severe repairLabel among triggered gates. A safety-severity flag forces reject_visual_plan. Ladder read top-down, first match (ruling 2).
